category_summary:
**NVIDIA's GTC Taipei** dominated the day, anchored by **Jensen Huang's** [keynote](/?date=2026-06-02&category=social#item-fae927d7a336) and the **Cosmos 3** [open physical-AI omnimodel release](/?date=2026-06-02&category=social#item-115a1c2de163).

- **NVIDIA** unveiled the **RTX Spark** [1-petaflop personal superchip](/?date=2026-06-02&category=social#item-768d66a39695) (with **Microsoft**) and the production-ready **Vera Rubin** [pod-scale platform](/?date=2026-06-02&category=social#item-fcec0b1c8604), signaling a push into both personal and data-center AI compute.
- **Cosmos 3** [launched fully open](/?date=2026-06-02&category=social#item-c9016362e2ce) (weights + recipes on Hugging Face), with [day-0 **vLLM** support](/?date=2026-06-02&category=social#item-2ff23346011b) and benchmark-leadership claims; the [**Cosmos Coalition**](/?date=2026-06-02&category=social#item-217123a1068c) (joined by **Runway**) frames an open world-model ecosystem.
- **Nathan Lambert** [cast NVIDIA as the tip of the spear](/?date=2026-06-02&category=social#item-758838745fdf) for US open models, while **Nemotron 3 Ultra** was [teased for this week](/?date=2026-06-02&category=social#item-ff796db06666).

Major corporate moves drew heavy engagement: **Anthropic** [confidentially filed a draft S-1](/?date=2026-06-02&category=social#item-3c7fcdfd4794) with the SEC, opening the door to an IPO, and **OpenAI** made its frontier models and Codex [generally available on **AWS Bedrock**](/?date=2026-06-02&category=social#item-09d05995cdca).

- **Andrew Ng** [spotlighted the rise](/?date=2026-06-02&category=social#item-81ad9fdd7384) of the **AI Forward Deployed Engineer**, arguing AI is creating roles, not just erasing them.
- **Perplexity** [introduced **Search as Code**](/?date=2026-06-02&category=social#item-1e166789ffa3), an agent architecture that writes Python over its search stack to cut latency and context pollution.
- On policy and economics, **Bernie Sanders** [floated a 50% one-time stock tax](/?date=2026-06-02&category=social#item-11f1a71b7bea) on **OpenAI, Anthropic, and xAI**, while **Gary Marcus** [predicted a 2026 AI-bubble reckoning](/?date=2026-06-02&category=social#item-71aaeb865aad).

items.title: One of the new, buzzy jobs in Silicon Valley is the AI Forward Deployed Engineer (FDE), an engineer ...
items.content:
One of the new, buzzy jobs in Silicon Valley is the AI Forward Deployed Engineer (FDE), an engineer who is embedded within a client organization to help customize solutions, such as building and tuning agentic workflows that suit the client’s particular needs. I’ve heard from people who are wondering anew about the FDE career path since OpenAI and Anthropic started building new teams to place FDEs within client organizations.

The rise of FDEs for AI workloads is one way AI is creating new jobs (and why the jobpolcalypse narrative of upcoming job market collapse is false -- there will be many AI and non-AI jobs). However, I believe there will be far more AI Engineer jobs than FDEs, as I explain below.

The FDE role was pioneered about two decades ago by Palantir, which sent engineers to government locations to work on secure, air-gapped networks. In addition to having good technical skills, FDEs need communication skills and sometimes business skills. For example, they may need to speak with clients to understand their needs, formulate a strategy to prioritize projects, explain complex technology, and respectfully push back if a client asks for something unrealistic. They’re enjoying a resurgence because of the amount of work involved in taking an off-the-shelf LLM and building it into a custom agentic workflow that fits particular business needs.

However, I believe the number of AI Engineer jobs will be far larger. A company might accept a few FDEs to be embedded within its organization. But most companies will want far more of their own employees working on their projects. While my organizations do hire FDEs, we hire far more AI Engineers! Also, a common client concern is that it is hard to find vendor-neutral FDEs — they are, after all, there to deeply integrate a particular vendor’s product into a company. In this moment when it’s hard to predict which AI service will be the best one in a year’s time, optionality (the ability to pick whatever vendor turns out to fit best in the future) is very valuable. In contrast, letting FDEs tightly bind a company’s processes significantly reduces optionality.

Right now, I see surging demand for AI Engineers who can build software applications using AI software components (like LLM prompting, agentic frameworks, evals, etc.) and effectively use AI coding agents (like Claude Code, Codex, Antigravity CLI, and OpenCode). As the AI Engineer role matures, I expect it to fragment into more specialized roles, like the generic Software Engineer role from decades ago fragmented into frontend, backend, mobile, data engineering, devops, and so on.

What will be the future, specialized AI engineering roles? I don’t know. Perhaps there will be AI FDEs, LLMOps Engineers, Evals Engineers, AI Data Engineers, Harness Engineers, and other roles we don’t have names for yet. But for now, I see a lot of AI engineers who are generalists create a lot of value. Skilled AI Engineers are in very high demand! As our field continues to mature over the coming decade, I look forward to new specializations within AI Engineering that create even more job opportunities.

[Original text: The Batch newsletter]

items.title: The NVIDIA Vera Rubin platform is a multi-rack pod-scale system, built to process agentic AI and is ...
items.content:
The NVIDIA Vera Rubin platform is a multi-rack pod-scale system, built to process agentic AI and is now in full production.

Built through extreme co-design, Vera Rubin unifies five connected rack-scale systems — the NVIDIA Vera Rubin NVL72, Vera CPU rack, Groq 3 LPX, Vera BlueField-4 STX, and Spectrum-6 SPX Ethernet — all purpose-built for global-scale agentic AI infrastructure. 
 
Vera Rubin delivers intelligence at scale, with the performance, efficiency and security needed to power the next industrial revolution.
 
Congratulations to @Microsoft, @Dell, and @CoreWeave for each standing up their Vera Rubin NVL72 engineering racks.
 
Learn more: https://t.co/HIlpgACFll

items.reasoning: Major data-center hardware announcement with strong engagement and named hyperscaler partners; central infrastructure news for agentic AI.

items.title: U.S. senator Bernie Sanders just said he will introduce a bill that would put a one-time 50% tax on ...
items.content:
U.S. senator Bernie Sanders just said he will introduce a bill that would put a one-time 50% tax on OpenAI, Anthropic, and xAI paid in stock, not cash.

The government would get voting shares and an equal seat on each company's board, and the stock would fund a public fund owned by citizens.

He pointed to Norway, whose oil-funded sovereign wealth fund is now worth more than $2T, and Alaska, which built one from oil revenues 50 years ago and pays annual dividends to residents.

He also noted the AI companies have already floated their own versions: OpenAI proposed a "public wealth fund," and Anthropic's "national sovereign wealth funds with stakes in A.I."

Sanders: "A.I. is being built on a public resource far more valuable than oil: the accumulated knowledge, creativity and labor of mankind."

This’ll propel OpenAI/Anthropic to huge valuations soon, while the open model economy slowly grows to be bigger & enable the long-tail of use.

items.title:
just a small zoom out on the vibe shift:

in Feb 2025 @soumithchintala was talking about his dream o...
items.content:
just a small zoom out on the vibe shift:

in Feb 2025 @soumithchintala was talking about his dream of personal, local, private agents, most people didn't believe him.

it's June 2026 and @pewdiepie has just released his vibecoded @opencode wrapper that is a complete personal AI productivity suite including email, docs, and calendar. top of HN, easily >1m views, >10k stars in a day.

if your Knowledge Work Agents startup can't beat pewdiepie you might as well pack up and go home at this point, his is the benchmark for what you can DIY.

items.title:
This pod was an incredible gift to the community: 

not only our first pod about @xAI, but Ethan rea...
items.content:
This pod was an incredible gift to the community: 

not only our first pod about @xAI, but Ethan really indulged on all our questions on how to train a SOTA Videogen world model, including specific areas (consistent extending/editing, voice) that Grok  @Imagine is *still* SOTA,

on top of the factual overviews he ALSO came loaded with opinions/predictions:

- why he's quitting Videogen for LLMs: video models get most of their intelligence from LLMs, not from scaling video data
- why the next frontier for videogen also happens to be video agent models - agentic models trained to orchestrate video models
- why deterministic compression (like MP4) is a useless target vs VAE compression
- Videomaxxing: if you truly believe in the "Moore's law" of AI/genmedia, then video models become the final boss UI of everything, like Flipbook (below)
items.author: @swyx
items.summary: swyx recaps a podcast with an xAI researcher on training SOTA videogen world models, arguing video models get intelligence from LLMs not video scaling, that video agent models are the next frontier, and that VAE compression beats deterministic compression.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical insights and predictions from a credible commentator with strong engagement; meaningful for world-model/genmedia direction.
items.summary_html: swyx recaps a podcast with an xAI researcher on training SOTA videogen world models, arguing video models get intelligence from LLMs not video scaling, that video agent models are the next frontier, and that VAE compression beats deterministic compression.
items.content_html:
This pod was an incredible gift to the community:

not only our first pod about @xAI, but Ethan really indulged on all our questions on how to train a SOTA Videogen world model, including specific areas (consistent extending/editing, voice) that Grok @Imagine is *still* SOTA,

on top of the factual overviews he ALSO came loaded with opinions/predictions:

  • why he's quitting Videogen for LLMs: video models get most of their intelligence from LLMs, not from scaling video data
  • why the next frontier for videogen also happens to be video agent models - agentic models trained to orchestrate video models
  • why deterministic compression (like MP4) is a useless target vs VAE compression
  • Videomaxxing: if you truly believe in the "Moore's law" of AI/genmedia, then video models become the final boss UI of everything, like Flipbook (below)

items.title:
LLMs only work for your business when they are subsidized by venture capital.

With the current pric...
items.content:
LLMs only work for your business when they are subsidized by venture capital.

With the current prices per token, you cannot build anything AI-based and sell it for a fair price customers would be willing to pay.

Customers will not be willing to pay the fair price because they are accustomed to paying unfair prices for heavily subsidized, subscription-based chatbots.

When the venture capital dries up and the subscription-based chatbots are no longer heavily subsidized, customers will feel the shock of finding out what it really costs to make an LLM "think" for minutes before making a decision or providing an answer.

Until then, your non-heavily-subsidized business with fair prices will have few to no clients.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Andriy Burkov argues LLMs only work for businesses while subsidized by venture capital, and that customers will be shocked by true costs once subsidies dry up.
items.reasoning: Substantive and provocative argument about AI cost sustainability from a known author with strong engagement.

items.title:
Last week we revamped Liteparse to be the fastest PDF parser out there ⚡️

An underrated part of lit...
items.content:
Last week we revamped Liteparse to be the fastest PDF parser out there ⚡️

An underrated part of liteparse is it doesn't just give you text. It gives you bounding boxes that a coding agent can use to paint exact audit trails back to the source document.

For instance, check out the deep research skill we compiled in liteparse_samples: https://t.co/eoICBmvEnq

Come check out liteparse: https://t.co/JNER0mVcB8

We are hard at work making liteparse even better (e.g. Markdown support). Please feel free to open up issues, PRs, and let us know your feature requests 🙏

items.title:
My mind is blown.

I invited my bot Otto to my Revenue Cat account, it checked email, created an acc...
items.content:
My mind is blown.

I invited my bot Otto to my Revenue Cat account, it checked email, created an account, accepted the invite, set up the project, connected to App Store Connect, set up products and pricing, created a paywall, integrated the whole thing to the iOS app, and is now creating a sandbox tester account for me.

All driven by just claude code + prompting + cli tools + peekaboo via Telegram.

I'm watching it live just for fun, but I could have gone to the gym and come back with the in-app purchase fully implemented.

Crazy time to be a startup founder.

items.title:
This was right five years ago, and still is:

“Large scale pretrained models are certainly likely to...
items.content:
This was right five years ago, and still is:

“Large scale pretrained models are certainly likely to figure prominently in artificial intelligence for the near future, and play an important role in commercial AI for some time to come. The results that have been achieved with them are certainly intriguing and it is worthwhile pursuing them. But it is unwise to assume that these techniques will suffice for AI in general. It may be an effective short-term research strategy to focus on the immediate challenges that seem to be surmountable, but focusing on the surmountable may not get us to what is most necessary: a firm foundation for reliably integrating statistics and machine learning with reasoning, knowledge, common sense and human values.”

Gary Marcus and Ernest Davis, 2021, “Has AI found a new Foundation?” at https://t.co/9VqBTg0FtK

I am excited to have a discussion with @djrosent and @gilbert from @AcquiredFM (my favorite podcast by a mile) in NYC on June 24. You can register to attend in person at the link below.

We're discussing "The AI Paradox" - all the apparent contradictions of AI (If AI is displacing jobs, why are there more  job postings for software engineers today than there were a few years ago? If AI is deflationary, when will see lower prices? If AI is destroying the software industry, why are some younger applied AI companies growing so quickly?). David and Ben are the smartest and most well researched interviewers on the planet, and I'm really looking forward to our discussion. 

https://t.co/Kuad1ek1CA

Current AI is bandaids all the way down; LLMs can’t play nicely with basic tools like databases and knowledge graphs, and you never know what you are going to get.

It’s long time to face facts: LLMs have their uses, but we need a sounder foundation for AI.

items.title: @GaryMarcus @geoffreyhinton I think the biggest issue here is that we don't really know how to diffe...
items.content:
@GaryMarcus @geoffreyhinton I think the biggest issue here is that we don't really know how to differentiate words from ideas, because we, humans, can only exchange ideas through words.

So, when an LLM generates words and a human generates words, and those are hard to tell from one another, how can we be sure that the LLM and the human aren't equivalent?

While knowing the math of LLMs helps to intuitively not equate the two, the experience with real humans also makes some (including myself) believe that the processes leading to words in many humans' heads look more like shallow pattern matching than deep understanding and thinking.

Not all humans are shallow pattern matchers, while all LLMs are, and this is what really differs them. But should we compare LLMs to *all* humans, or to the *majority* of them? If the majority is OK as the objective, then LLMs and a typical human might as well be both shallow pattern matchers.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Andriy Burkov argues we cannot easily distinguish words from ideas, suggesting that since many humans may be shallow pattern matchers, LLMs and a typical human might be comparable depending on the human baseline used.
items.reasoning: Substantive philosophical argument on LLM cognition from a known author with modest engagement.
items.summary_html: Andriy Burkov argues we cannot easily distinguish words from ideas, suggesting that since many humans may be shallow pattern matchers, LLMs and a typical human might be comparable depending on the human baseline used.
items.content_html:
@GaryMarcus @geoffreyhinton I think the biggest issue here is that we don't really know how to differentiate words from ideas, because we, humans, can only exchange ideas through words.

So, when an LLM generates words and a human generates words, and those are hard to tell from one another, how can we be sure that the LLM and the human aren't equivalent?

While knowing the math of LLMs helps to intuitively not equate the two, the experience with real humans also makes some (including myself) believe that the processes leading to words in many humans' heads look more like shallow pattern matching than deep understanding and thinking.

Not all humans are shallow pattern matchers, while all LLMs are, and this is what really differs them. But should we compare LLMs to *all* humans, or to the *majority* of them? If the majority is OK as the objective, then LLMs and a typical human might as well be both shallow pattern matchers.

items.title: Back in the Armadillo Aerospace days, our liquid oxygen loadouts were 50 to 100 gallons, and the tan...
items.content: Back in the Armadillo Aerospace days, our liquid oxygen loadouts were 50 to 100 gallons, and the tanks only weighed about a tenth of the propellant, so cooldown effects didn’t dominate. I have an effort going on now dealing with several-gram quantities of multiple cryogenic fluids, and it is an enormous pain when your valves and plumbing mass tens of times the fluid you are trying to deal with. I wish there was a viable cryogenic peristaltic pump!

items.title:
Microsoft made BI tools obsolete with one open-source AI visualization tool. 

Most analytics tools ...
items.content:
Microsoft made BI tools obsolete with one open-source AI visualization tool. 

Most analytics tools lock you into expensive seats and steep learning curves. 

Charts require SQL, transformations require code, dashboards require a specialist.
 
Microsoft Research just open-sourced Data Formulator, a tool that flips that workflow. 

You drag fields onto a canvas, type what you want in plain english. 

An AI agent builds the chart, transforms the data, and writes the query behind it.
 
The interface blends clicks with conversation. 

Ask a question, branch into a new exploration, or backtrack through a visual history of every step.
 
Key things it handles:
 
> Pulls data from screenshots
> Connects Postgres, MySQL, S3
> Plugs into OpenAI, Claude, Ollama
> Runs fully local if needed
> Saves sessions and shareable reports
 
Every chart exposes the underlying code, so nothing stays a black box.

items.title:
The marketplace is called "The Grid" and it just launched.

The idea is simple:

• You pick a qualit...
items.content:
The marketplace is called "The Grid" and it just launched.

The idea is simple:

• You pick a quality tier (standard, prime, max)
• The Grid routes your requests
• You get the cheapest model within your tier

You don't pick a model. You pick the tier and the market fills it at the best available price.

They audit suppliers against benchmarks. If a model drifts below the threshold for its tier, they pull it from the eligible set.

Basically, they are trying to ensure you never get cheap junk.

They have an OpenAI-compatible API, so you literally have to change one line of code to start using them. And if you don't like it, undo the change and you are right back with your previous vendor.

New accounts get the first 200M tokens free. Worth a look if you're spending real money on inference.

Here is the link to check them out: https://t.co/1axxs63XrC

Thanks to the team for partnering with me on this post.

items.content:
@GaryMarcus That's because paid AI influencers are running around and screaming WILD! Those CEOs (most them aren't very smart) think that everyone else but them knows how to use AI, so they are just trying hard to learn to do that right so that it becomes WILD for them too. When it doesn't work and their staff tells them that it doesn't work, they fire the negative staff and promote the positive ones.

This loop will run as long as the crazy venture capital is enough to cover the GPU expenses and the WILD-screaming AI influencers.

items.title:
The thing slowing down every AI agent just got 208× faster. 

For years, speeding up AI meant making...
items.content:
The thing slowing down every AI agent just got 208× faster. 

For years, speeding up AI meant making the language model itself run faster.

But agents changed the bottleneck. 

They lean on older tools like clustering and search to verify their work.

These classical operators were built for old hardware. They became the slow part.

FlashLib rebuilds them for modern GPUs. It is open-source and available now.

The speedups over the previous best library are large:

> TruncatedSVD runs 208 times faster
> Exact t-SNE hits 147 times
> MultinomialNB reaches 49 times
> KMeans climbs to 26 times

How? Each operation was rewritten to avoid wasteful memory use. 

The math stays identical.

You can also set a precision budget. Looser tolerance routes to a faster path.

The standout feature predicts cost before running. 

It estimates runtime and memory in five microseconds, no GPU needed.

So an agent can plan a whole pipeline. Will it know what to skip?

Super-fast CLI tool for moving data around:

$ ingestr ingest --source-uri --dest-uri

Ingestr is open-source, requires almost no setup, and it can move data from any source to any destination.

Supports every database you can imagine, data warehouses, and even SaaS applications.

Give it to Claude Code or Codex, and they will now transfer data at lighting speeds.

This thing is really fast!

items.title:
First consumer announcement from NVIDIA in a while.

"Running the Hermes harness," the video just sa...
items.content:
First consumer announcement from NVIDIA in a while.

"Running the Hermes harness," the video just said.

How consumer has changed. But the consumer doesn't know what @NousResearch's Hermes is. 

You do because you are on X. 

I do, because I had its CTO on my audio space a couple of months ago. 

But the consumer doesn't yet know how to spell "AI agents."

So, the question is, when will they? 

Maybe the wrong question. 

You and I will buy one. And do a raft of new things with it.

items.title:
A new learning curriculum on @ChapterPal: Prep reading for the ad ranking and targeting interview

T...
items.content:
A new learning curriculum on @ChapterPal: Prep reading for the ad ranking and targeting interview

This curriculum provides a comprehensive foundation for understanding the mechanics and machine learning challenges essential to ad ranking, targeting, and recommendation systems.

It begins with the economic principles of generalized second-price auctions before transitioning into the practical application of click-through rate prediction at industrial scale.

The reading list balances classical methodology with modern neural network architectures, covering techniques for feature interaction, sequence modeling, and multi-task learning for complex conversion objectives.

Furthermore, the selection addresses critical real-world engineering issues, including managing data bias, handling sparse categorical inputs, and utilizing counterfactual inference to improve model performance from logged bandit feedback.

https://t.co/s2SCx1X0Uf

items.title:
Nvidia just made some major moves at COMPUTEX, hitting several different layers of computing.

Some ...
items.content:
Nvidia just made some major moves at COMPUTEX, hitting several different layers of computing.

Some of the biggest annoucements:

1. RTX Spark is a new class of AI superchips NVIDIA built with Microsoft to run AI agents directly on Windows computers instead of in the cloud. 

Nvidia describes the shift as going "from tool to teammate" and "the first Windows computers purpose-built for personal agents." 

Built-in support for agents like Hermes and OpenClaw. Shipping this fall.

2. Vera, what Nvidia calls "the first CPU built for AI agents." 1.8x faster than rival CPU options. 

Jensen Huang: "AI agents will be the largest users of computing. Vera is the first CPU designed for that future — built to run agentic AI at hyperscale with extraordinary performance, efficiency and programmability." 

The New York Stock Exchange, which moves more than 1.1T messages a day, plans to use it to scale capacity. Also available this fall.

3. Cosmos 3, an open AI model that lets a robot or self-driving car understand a scene and predict what's about to happen before it moves. 

Can be used for vision tasks, as a world model simulator, or for robotics training. Unlike the other two, Cosmos 3 is already available now. 

4. Nemotron 3 Ultra - Nvidia's new 550B parameter open weight model coming this week. Tops U.S. open models and direclty competes with top options lke Kimi K2.6, GLM 5.1, Qwen 3.5 and others.

Ingestr supports 4 incremental strategies: replace, append, merge, and delete + insert.

To install it:
$ pip install ingestr

To move data:
$ ingestr ingest --source-uri --dest-uri

When I started I was broke and didn't have or want funding (still don't)

My only way to get the wor...
items.content:
When I started I was broke and didn't have or want funding (still don't)

My only way to get the word out about my websites was tell my story on here and on my blog

But if I told a basic normal story nobody would care

So you need something special

Back then I lucked out kinda because doing startups without funding was special (now it's normal) and doing it from your laptop while backpacking was special (now it's normal too) so nobody will care now if you do that

But you can find a modern unique edge to make your story interesting, you can see interesting stories on here go viral every day!

items.title:
You all sure post a lot. 

The AI industry, alone, posts about 30,000 times every day here on X. 

M...
items.content:
You all sure post a lot. 

The AI industry, alone, posts about 30,000 times every day here on X. 

My AI reads it all and builds this: https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ

Now my database has about a million posts in it. 

So now I can "talk" to a million of your posts. 

Which is pretty crazy. I can develop reports for companies that no one can do unless they have built the same.

All built on my lists: https://t.co/9eRY65x3IQ which I've been building for 19 years here. 

Highly curated data sources is the secret sauce for having AI develop highly personalized news systems. 

Thanks to @blevlabs for building the infrastructure and my agent, which reads and writes about the news better than, say, Anthropic can.
items.author: @Scobleizer

items.summary_html: Robert Scoble explains his AI agent reads roughly 30,000 daily AI-industry posts on X, has amassed a million-post database, and lets him generate custom reports, crediting blevlabs for the infrastructure.
items.content_html:
You all sure post a lot.

The AI industry, alone, posts about 30,000 times every day here on X.

My AI reads it all and builds this: https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ

Now my database has about a million posts in it.

So now I can "talk" to a million of your posts.

Which is pretty crazy. I can develop reports for companies that no one can do unless they have built the same.

All built on my lists: https://t.co/9eRY65x3IQ which I've been building for 19 years here.

Highly curated data sources is the secret sauce for having AI develop highly personalized news systems.

Thanks to @blevlabs for building the infrastructure and my agent, which reads and writes about the news better than, say, Anthropic can.

items.title:
I’ve talked to a lot of CIOs about AI strategy.
The conversation usually centers on models, vendors,...
items.content:
I’ve talked to a lot of CIOs about AI strategy.
The conversation usually centers on models, vendors, and use cases. It almost never centers on the network.

That’s the gap.

Network infrastructure plays a major role in how AI performs at scale.

Under load. At scale. 

The PGA Championship is 300+ acres of simultaneous data streams hitting one system.

That’s not a controlled environment. That’s a stress test.

And it held.

items.summary_html: Argues CIO AI strategy conversations overlook network infrastructure, citing the PGA Championship as a stress test.
items.content_html:
I’ve talked to a lot of CIOs about AI strategy.

The conversation usually centers on models, vendors, and use cases. It almost never centers on the network.

That’s the gap.

Network infrastructure plays a major role in how AI performs at scale.

Under load. At scale.

The PGA Championship is 300+ acres of simultaneous data streams hitting one system.

That’s not a controlled environment. That’s a stress test.

And it held.

→ Powered by T-Mobile’s 5G network, fragmented data across 300+ acres is transmitted at the speed AI inference actually requires

This isn’t a sports story. It’s an enterprise story.

Hospitals. Airports. Campuses.

Every one of them has the same problem: fragmented data, delayed decisions.

The architecture here solves for that.

items.title:
Picture this.

300+ acres. Thousands of fans. Dozens of systems running simultaneously.

Ticketing. ...
items.content:
Picture this.

300+ acres. Thousands of fans. Dozens of systems running simultaneously.

Ticketing. POS. Gate cameras. SuperMobile. An AI-enabled, autonomous robot. All feeding operational data into different systems.

The traditional answer? More dashboards. More analysts. More lag.

The AI answer? Unify it into real-time operational visibility and let the system surface patterns earlier in the process.

But here’s the catch most people miss:
→ That only works if the network supports responsive decision-making

That model helped power computers, smartphones, cloud, AI, and the digital economy.

items.title:
@flowersslop and see Marcus and Davis, all the way back in 2021: 

“Large scale pretrained models ar...
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Gary Marcus repeats his 2021 quote about the limits of large pretrained models in a reply.
items.reasoning: Duplicate of prior content with low engagement.
items.summary_html: Gary Marcus repeats his 2021 quote about the limits of large pretrained models in a reply.
items.content_html:
@flowersslop and see Marcus and Davis, all the way back in 2021:

“Large scale pretrained models are certainly likely to figure prominently in artificial intelligence for the near future, and play an important role in commercial AI for some time to come. The results that have been achieved with them are certainly intriguing and it is worthwhile pursuing them. But it is unwise to assume that these techniques will suffice for AI in general. It may be an effective short-term research strategy to focus on the immediate challenges that seem to be surmountable, but focusing on the surmountable may not get us to what is most necessary: a firm foundation for reliably integrating statistics and machine learning with reasoning, knowledge, common sense and human values.”

https://t.co/tkWXP82zqu

LogicFolding is one example.

Think of moving from a single-story building to a multi-story building.

Circuits can be arranged closer together, connected faster, and designed with shorter paths.

That can help reduce delay and improve efficiency.

items.title:
The idea is simple, but important.

Instead of only scaling geometry, scale time.

Reduce the time i...
items.content:
The idea is simple, but important.

Instead of only scaling geometry, scale time.

Reduce the time it takes for signals, data, and instructions to move through devices, circuits, chips, and full systems.

Performance is not only about size.

It is also about movement.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Argues the Tau Scaling idea is to scale time by reducing signal latency rather than only shrinking geometry.
items.reasoning: Interesting reframing of scaling but sponsored with minimal reach.

Reduce the time it takes for signals, data, and instructions to move through devices, circuits, chips, and full systems.

Performance is not only about size.

It is also about movement.

items.title:
New release from @PacktDataML at https://t.co/WyEqNc93yl

"A Practical Guide to Reinforcement Learni...
items.content:
New release from @PacktDataML at https://t.co/WyEqNc93yl

"A Practical Guide to Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)"

𝗔𝗺𝗮𝘇𝗼𝗻 𝘀𝘂𝗺𝗺𝗮𝗿𝘆:

RLHF is a powerful approach to AI alignment and human-centered machine learning. By combining reinforcement learning algorithms with human feedback signals, RLHF has become a key method for improving the safety, reliability, and alignment of large language models (LLMs).

This book begins with the foundations of reinforcement learning and policy optimization, including algorithms such as proximal policy optimization (PPO), and explains how reward models and human preference learning help fine-tune AI systems and generative AI models.
items.author: @KirkDBorne
items.summary: Borne promotes a practical guide to RLHF, summarizing its role in AI alignment and LLM fine-tuning via PPO and reward models.
items.reasoning: Relevant alignment/RLHF educational content but promotional book post.

items.content_html:

𝗔𝗺𝗮𝘇𝗼𝗻 𝘀𝘂𝗺𝗺𝗮𝗿𝘆:

RLHF is a powerful approach to AI alignment and human-centered machine learning. By combining reinforcement learning algorithms with human feedback signals, RLHF has become a key method for improving the safety, reliability, and alignment of large language models (LLMs).

This book begins with the foundations of reinforcement learning and policy optimization, including algorithms such as proximal policy optimization (PPO), and explains how reward models and human preference learning help fine-tune AI systems and generative AI models.

This is full variational Bayes tho, not EM.

It enables:

→ 360 degree swing analysis
→ Real-time broadcast-quality content delivery
→ Cameras that follow the talent, not the other way around

That last point is the shift.

Production starts to become mobile by default.

items.title:
Live production has always been shaped by infrastructure.

Where can the cameras go?

Where can the ...
items.content:
Live production has always been shaped by infrastructure.

Where can the cameras go?

Where can the crew connect?

How fast can footage move from capture to distribution?

At the Range Show, 5G On Demand changes that equation:

→ No fixed infrastructure
→ No visible cables
→ Broadcast-quality delivery from wherever the action is
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Argues live production has always been infrastructure-limited and 5G On Demand removes fixed cabling constraints.

items.title: @AndyXAndersen Renting a single 8-GPU node for a year costs about $200,000/year, and you cannot run ...
items.content: @AndyXAndersen Renting a single 8-GPU node for a year costs about $200,000/year, and you cannot run much on a single node. The real cost numbers are crazy when you start doing the math. Only when you are Google with your own TPUs and deep pockets, or you are Anthropic/OpenAI with wild venture money, can you offer a monthly subscription for a price that doesn't make you run away in agony. I'm not sure if $100/month or $500/month would make any business sense; maybe for some clients and use cases they would.

items.title: @ifaposto Hmm well so in the E-step we update q(Z|X) to maximize the ELBO Eq[log p(X,Z)/q(Z|X)] wher...
items.content: @ifaposto Hmm well so in the E-step we update q(Z|X) to maximize the ELBO Eq[log p(X,Z)/q(Z|X)] where the maximizer is q(Z|X)=p(Z|X) at each step. So in your particular example, I would think  we would just learn w=0, since m is already the optimal update.

items.title: I’m gonna delete this thread because I think people complaining about airlines on Twitter are pretty...
items.content:
I’m gonna delete this thread because I think people complaining about airlines on Twitter are pretty lame, including myself!

That said, I think the takeaway is that the reason I was upset is precisely because of dashed expectations. If this were American I would have shrugged my shoulders. So I guess that’s a compliment to United, but also a reminder that it’s risky to advertise things (like Starlink) when you can’t yet confidently deliver on them. 

Anyhow, 28B on Southwest. Let’s go! 😅
items.author: @benthompson

items.title: @united I don’t need vouchers or miles. The reason to have status is for when bad things happen, and...
items.content:
@united I don’t need vouchers or miles. The reason to have status is for when bad things happen, and the ball was dropped in several ways:

- United texted that the plane was boarding when in fact it was not. The flight then continued to show “on time” and “boarding” until it finally started boarding 45 minutes after scheduled departure time.
- Because the status was never updated, the 1K desk would not put me on the next flight; by the time the status updated the flight — which previously had open seats — was now full, so I was on Standby (and did not get on).
- I was booked on the flight after that, in economy, despite first class being open and having a first class ticket. 
- At this point the sprint through the airport happened 
- I booked an alternative flight on another carrier; the service desk said I would not get a refund, and if I appealed, it would only be for an economy seat 

I get that bad things happen. However, I’ve been the exact kind of customer you want: I go to https://t.co/YOFWJ323JF to book and don’t look elsewhere. That’s what is going to change. I’ll still fly you a lot given your network fits my needs (although I’ll definitely be back on Eva for Pacific flights), but I’ve been 1K for customer service, not perks, and that just didn’t work out at all today. So I don’t think this conversation will be fruitful.
items.author: @benthompson
items.summary: Ben Thompson details a list of service failures by United during a delayed/missed flight.
items.reasoning: Detailed but entirely off-topic customer-service complaint; author credibility doesn't make it AI-relevant.
items.summary_html: Ben Thompson details a list of service failures by United during a delayed/missed flight.
items.content_html:
@united I don’t need vouchers or miles. The reason to have status is for when bad things happen, and the ball was dropped in several ways:

  • United texted that the plane was boarding when in fact it was not. The flight then continued to show “on time” and “boarding” until it finally started boarding 45 minutes after scheduled departure time.
  • Because the status was never updated, the 1K desk would not put me on the next flight; by the time the status updated the flight — which previously had open seats — was now full, so I was on Standby (and did not get on).
  • I was booked on the flight after that, in economy, despite first class being open and having a first class ticket.
  • At this point the sprint through the airport happened
  • I booked an alternative flight on another carrier; the service desk said I would not get a refund, and if I appealed, it would only be for an economy seat

I get that bad things happen. However, I’ve been the exact kind of customer you want: I go to https://t.co/YOFWJ323JF to book and don’t look elsewhere. That’s what is going to change. I’ll still fly you a lot given your network fits my needs (although I’ll definitely be back on Eva for Pacific flights), but I’ve been 1K for customer service, not perks, and that just didn’t work out at all today. So I don’t think this conversation will be fruitful.
